--- title: "2023 NFL Losers Pool" editor: source description: Simulating NFL games for competing in a Losers Pool. categories: - R - Sports Analytics - Monte Carlo format: html: code-fold: true code-tools: true --- I am competing in another NFL Loser's Pool this year, and like past years I will be trying to use R to gain an advantage against my friends who are competing in the pool. You can read about the approach I took last year here: [NFL Losers Pool - 2022](https://www.jordanhutchings.com/blog/2022-09-07-nfl-losers-pool-2022/). Given Disney's purchase of fivethirtyeight, the main source of my season prediction data, I've decided to look into other methods of making ELO predictions. Specifically, I came across the `nflseedR` package, which appears to use ELO ratings to simulate the season - similar to the process done by fivethirtyeight. However, you need to provide the elo ratings to the model first. ## Generating Season Projections Let's first collect the NFL season data using `nflseedR`. Notice we have the week, date, teams playing, and some of the betting markets data for each game. This function is a wrapper to the `nflreadr::load_schedules()` function. Here is a link to the [data dictionary](https://nflreadr.nflverse.com/articles/dictionary_schedules.html). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} pacman::p_load(nflseedR, kableExtra, dplyr, ggplot2) # setup params theme_set( theme_bw(10) + theme( # panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), axis.ticks.y = element_blank(), plot.title=element_text(size = 12, hjust = 0), plot.title.position = "plot", plot.subtitle=element_text(face="italic", size=12, margin=margin(b=12)), plot.caption=element_text(size=8, color="#7a7d7e"), legend.position = "right", legend.background = element_blank(), legend.key = element_blank(), strip.background = element_rect(fill = "#2C3E50"), strip.text = element_text(color = "white"), axis.text.x=element_text(angle = -30, hjust = 0) ) ) UPDATE_SIMS = FALSE data = load_sharpe_games() # get 538 elo data at last point in time elo_raw = data.table::fread("https://projects.fivethirtyeight.com/nfl-api/nfl_elo_latest.csv") # write.csv(elo_raw, file = "538_elo.csv") elo = rbind(elo_raw |> select(date, team = team1, elo = qbelo1_post), elo_raw |> select(date, team = team2, elo = qbelo2_post)) |> group_by(team) |> arrange(date) |> slice(1L) |> select(-date) |> ungroup() # update team names elo = elo |> mutate(team = case_when(team == "OAK" ~ "LV", team == "LAR" ~ "LA", team == "WSH" ~ "WAS", TRUE ~ team)) data = data |> left_join(elo |> rename(elo_home = elo), by = c("home_team" = "team")) |> left_join(elo |> rename(elo_away = elo), by = c("away_team" = "team")) tbl_data = data |> filter(season == 2023) |> select(week, gameday, away_team, home_team, elo_home, elo_away, spread_line) |> arrange(week) |> mutate(gameday = format(as.Date(gameday), "%B %e, %Y")) tbl_data |> kbl(caption = "2023 Season Data", col.names = c("Week", "Day", "Away", "Home", "ELO Home", "ELO Away", "Spread")) |> kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped") |> scroll_box(height = "500px", fixed_thead = TRUE) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}

````` ::: ::: Now that we have access to the data, lets run some season simulations. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # taking from the vingette: https://nflseedr.com/articles/nflsim.html elo_model <- function(teams, games, week_num, ...) { # round out (away from zero) # this way the simulator never simulates a tie # the simulator will still allow ties to be simulated if you want # ... but not on playoff games round_out <- function(x) { x[!is.na(x) & x < 0] <- floor(x[!is.na(x) & x < 0]) x[!is.na(x) & x > 0] <- ceiling(x[!is.na(x) & x > 0]) return(x) } # we're going to store elo as a new columns in the teams data # it won't start off there of course, so we need to determine it # from our arguments if (!("elo" %in% colnames(teams))) { args <- list(...) if ("elo" %in% names(args)) { # pull the elo info from custom arguments teams <- teams %>% dplyr::inner_join(args$elo %>% dplyr::select(team, elo), by = c("team" = "team")) } else { # error with a friendly error message if no elo data is passed in stop("Pass in a tibble `elo` as an argument to `simulate_nfl()`") } } # isolate the ratings data by sim and by team only # we will want to join to the games data later and don't want excess columns ratings <- teams %>% dplyr::select(sim, team, elo) # simulate game outcomes games <- games %>% # add in the away team's elo to the game data # note we join on both `sim` and the team # always join on `sim` to make sure each sim cares about only its data dplyr::inner_join(ratings, by = c("sim" = "sim", "away_team" = "team")) %>% dplyr::rename(away_elo = elo) %>% # repeat for the home team as well dplyr::inner_join(ratings, by = c("sim" = "sim", "home_team" = "team")) %>% dplyr::rename(home_elo = elo) %>% dplyr::mutate( # calculate the elo difference elo_diff = home_elo - away_elo, # add in a small HFA amount if played at home elo_diff = elo_diff + ifelse(location == "Home", 20, 0), # make an adjustment for rest elo_diff = elo_diff + (home_rest - away_rest) / 7 * 25, # playoff games swing elo more elo_diff = elo_diff * ifelse(game_type == "REG", 1, 1.2), # from elo, we calculate the home team's win percentage wp = 1 / (10^(-elo_diff / 400) + 1), # we also can calculate the estimate (mean points home team wins by) estimate = elo_diff / 25, result = dplyr::case_when( # !!! ALWAYS DO THIS NEXT LINE IN YOUR `result` CHANGES !!! # you have to make sure you're only changing unfinished games in current week # if you don't do this, it will usually error out on a friendly error message is.na(result) & week == week_num ~ as.integer(round_out(rnorm(n(), estimate, 13))), # if not this week or known result, leave as-is TRUE ~ as.integer(result) ), # simplify to 1 = win, 0 = loss, 0.5 = tie to help calculate elo shift outcome = dplyr::case_when( is.na(result) ~ NA_real_, result > 0 ~ 1, result < 0 ~ 0, TRUE ~ 0.5 ), # calculate the amount to adjust home team's elo by elo_input = dplyr::case_when( is.na(result) ~ NA_real_, result > 0 ~ elo_diff * 0.001 + 2.2, result < 0 ~ -elo_diff * 0.001 + 2.2, TRUE ~ 1.0, ), elo_mult = log(pmax(abs(result), 1) + 1.0) * 2.2 / elo_input, elo_shift = 20 * elo_mult * (outcome - wp) ) %>% # we don't want these columns in `games` any more # remove any columns you don't need when you're done # otherwise the next week they'll get joined as `col.x` and `col.y` # which will almost certainly break your script dplyr::select( -away_elo, -home_elo, -elo_diff, -wp, -estimate, -outcome, -elo_input, -elo_mult ) # apply elo shifts teams <- teams %>% # join games results from this week to away teams (within same sim!) # note this is a LEFT join, we don't want to remove any teams rows dplyr::left_join(games %>% dplyr::filter(week == week_num) %>% dplyr::select(sim, away_team, elo_shift), by = c("sim" = "sim", "team" = "away_team") ) %>% # away team's elo gets subtracted by elo amount # if the team wasn't an away team, do nothing dplyr::mutate(elo = elo - ifelse(!is.na(elo_shift), elo_shift, 0)) %>% # we don't want to keep `elo_shift` in `teams` either, remove it dplyr::select(-elo_shift) %>% # repeat the above except now do it for the home team dplyr::left_join(games %>% dplyr::filter(week == week_num) %>% dplyr::select(sim, home_team, elo_shift), by = c("sim" = "sim", "team" = "home_team") ) %>% # note that a team on a bye will have `elo_shift` as NA for both joins # this means it won't change, which is what we want dplyr::mutate(elo = elo + ifelse(!is.na(elo_shift), elo_shift, 0)) %>% dplyr::select(-elo_shift) # we need to keep `elo_shift` out of `games` too and we're done with it games <- games %>% dplyr::select(-elo_shift) # return the updated teams and games information # note that `teams` will now have an updated `elo` column which will # be used for the next week's games # note that starting `elo` values are the same per-team... # ... but after that will differ per sim depending on that sim's results return(list(teams = teams, games = games)) } # update with 538 elo initial_elo <- tibble::tibble( team = elo$team, elo = elo$elo ) if(UPDATE_SIMS) { #TODO: confirm this includes updating elo future::plan("multisession") sim = nflseedR::simulate_nfl( nfl_season = 2023, process_games = elo_model, elo = initial_elo, sim_include = "REG", simulations = 1234, sims_per_round = 4) write.csv(sim$game_summary, file = "sim-data.csv", row.names = FALSE) future::plan("sequential") sim = sim$game_summary } else { sim = read.csv("sim-data.csv") } sim |> select(week, away_team, home_team, away_percentage, home_percentage) |> kbl(title = "Simulation results") |> kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped") |> scroll_box(height = "500px", fixed_thead = TRUE) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}

````` ::: ::: Lets dig into these results a bit. What is the variation like in game outcomes? Notice the spread is nearly identical in the tails. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sim |> filter(week %in% 1:15) |> mutate(label = paste("Week", week), label = reorder(label, week)) |> ggplot(aes(x = result, color = away_team)) + geom_density(alpha = 0.2) + facet_wrap(.~label, nrow = 3) + scale_color_viridis_d() + theme(legend.position = "none", axis.ticks.y = element_blank(), axis.text.y = element_blank()) + labs(title = "Game point differential", subtitle = "Plotting densities of the point differential for each weekly game", x = "Differential", y = "Density") ``` ::: It looks like the outcomes of the game are heavily functional form dependent. We are drawing our RNG for the game based on a normal distribution with SD = 13, giving us a very similar spread across the season. ### Finalize projection data We can aggregate the simulation results to get us the estimated win rates for each game. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} game_projs = sim |> mutate(loser = case_when(away_percentage < home_percentage ~ away_team, away_percentage >= home_percentage ~ home_team), pct = case_when(away_percentage < home_percentage ~ away_percentage, away_percentage >= home_percentage ~ home_percentage)) |> select(week, loser, pct) rbind(sim |> select(week, team = home_team, pct = home_percentage), sim |> select(week, team = away_team, pct = away_percentage)) |> mutate(team = reorder(team, pct)) |> ggplot(aes(x = week, y = team, fill = pct)) + geom_tile(color = "white") + scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "B", labels = scales::percent_format()) + scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0), n.breaks = 18) + scale_y_discrete(expand = c(0, 0)) + theme(panel.grid = element_blank(), axis.title.y = element_blank()) + labs(title = "Projected Win Percent", x = "Week Number", fill = "Win Percent", caption = "Blank tiles are bye weeks.\nPercentages simulated from nflseedR::simulate_nfl().") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## Determining the optimal pick sequence Let's compare the greedy picking algorithm with the opportunity cost model. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # greedy algo ---- pick_lowest_per_week <- function(data) { results <- data.frame() # To store the selected rows selected_ids <- c() # To keep track of selected IDs selected_weeks <- c() # To keep track of selected weeks unique_weeks <- unique(data$week) for (week in unique_weeks) { week_data <- data[data$week == week, ] valid_rows <- week_data[!(week_data$ID %in% selected_ids) & !(week_data$week %in% selected_weeks), ] if (nrow(valid_rows) > 0) { min_value_row <- valid_rows[which.min(valid_rows$value), ] results <- rbind(results, min_value_row) selected_ids <- c(selected_ids, min_value_row$ID) selected_weeks <- c(selected_weeks, min_value_row$week) } } results |> rename(loser = ID, pct = value) } # oc algo ---- pick_max_distance <- function(data) { results <- data.frame() # To store the selected rows selected_ids <- c() # To keep track of selected IDs selected_weeks <- c() # To keep track of selected weeks while (nrow(data) > 0) { data = data |> arrange(week, value) valid_rows = valid_rows <- data[!(data$ID %in% selected_ids) & !(data$week %in% selected_weeks), ] valid_rows = valid_rows |> group_by(week) |> slice(1:2L) if (nrow(valid_rows) == 0) { break } distances <- outer(valid_rows$value, valid_rows$value, FUN = function(x, y) abs(x - y)) max_distance <- max(distances) max_indices <- which(distances == max_distance, arr.ind = TRUE) selected_index <- max_indices[1, 1] selected_row <- valid_rows[selected_index, ] results <- rbind(results, selected_row) selected_ids <- c(selected_ids, selected_row$ID) selected_weeks <- c(selected_weeks, selected_row$week) data <- data[-selected_index, ] } results |> rename(loser = ID, pct = value) } # past OC model ---- delta = function(week_num, df, picks) { df = data.table::setDT(df) tmp = arrange(df[week == week_num & !loser %in% picks$pick], pct) # sort and filter for a given week score = tmp[2, ]$pct - tmp[1, ]$pct # compute oc of best pick data.frame( pick = tmp[1]$loser, score = score, week = week_num, pct = tmp[1]$pct ) } picks_by_oc = function(NUM_WEEKS = 12, START_WEEK = 3) { picks = data.frame() for(i in START_WEEK:NUM_WEEKS) { weeks = seq(START_WEEK, NUM_WEEKS, 1)[!seq(START_WEEK, NUM_WEEKS, 1) %in% picks$week] rankings = lapply(weeks, function(x) delta(x, df=trunc_game_projs, picks)) |> bind_rows() pick = rankings[rank(-rankings$score) == 1, ] picks = rbind(picks, pick) } arrange(picks, week) } trunc_game_projs = game_projs |> filter(week %in% 3:13) picks_oc = picks_by_oc(12, 3) greedy_picks = pick_lowest_per_week(trunc_game_projs |> rename(ID = loser, value = pct)) oc_picks = pick_max_distance(trunc_game_projs |> rename(value = pct, ID = loser)) picks_oc = picks_by_oc(13, 3) # Plot results picks = rbind(picks_oc |> select(-score) |> rename(loser = pick) |> mutate(label = "OC"), greedy_picks |> mutate(label = "Greedy")) avg_pct = sprintf("Average win percent: Greedy %s%% | OC %s%%", round(mean(greedy_picks$pct), 4) * 100, round(mean(picks_oc$pct), 4) * 100) # make plot picks |> group_by(week) |> mutate(d = lag(pct) - pct, # compute difference between pick probs y_pos = (lag(pct) + pct)/2, # compute the positioning to be in the middle of picks y_label = ifelse(d == 0, "", paste0(round(d * 100, 1), "%")), loser = ifelse(y_label == "" & !is.na(y_label), "", loser)) |> # only show label for one of two identical losers ggplot(aes(x = week, y = pct, color = label, label = y_label)) + geom_line(aes(group = week), color="#e3e2e1", size = 2, alpha = 0.7) + geom_point(size = 3) + geom_text(aes(x = week, y = y_pos), color = "#414a4c", nudge_x = 0.4) + ggrepel::geom_text_repel(aes(x = week, y = pct, label = loser), nudge_x = 0.3, color = "#414a4c", direction = "y") + scale_color_viridis_d(option = "B") + scale_x_continuous(n.breaks = 12) + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(), n.breaks = 7) + labs(title = "Week-by-Week Win Probabilities across Models", subtitle = avg_pct, caption = "Differences are interepreted as the additional risk taken on by selecting the OC model.\nData is updated weekly, and so predictions in plot are subject to change.", x = "Week Number", y = "Win Probability", color = "Model") + theme(legend.position="bottom", legend.background = element_blank()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0. ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead. ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Warning: Removed 11 rows containing missing values (`geom_text()`). ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: The two models are pretty close to eachother. It looks like OC is a little more stable, but both give roughly a 30% average chance of being eliminated each week.
